sentences.text: Well, obviously, as long as you are at school, your friends are your school friends.
Or if you are going to a youth movement, you make some friends there.
And I've had some connection with the Mizrahi youth movement, called in those days [HEBREW], the equivalent of what we've got here B'nai Akiva.
But essentially, it-- through the duration of-- of-- of secondary school, it was my school friends that was my world.
It was my world.
Exclusively Jewish.

There wasn't-- it was a Hebrew gymnasium, where every-- every subject-- excuse me-- was taught in Hebrew, with the exception of Polish literature, Polish history, and Polish geography that were taught in Polish.
Everything else, including my Latin, was translated into Hebrew.
And my algebra and my-- everything was in Hebrew.
So of course, and not only that, but we were, by and large, of the same [POLISH].
The-- the social structure of the community, of the people who came there were, with some exceptions-- and the exception that proved the rule-- were of the same social-- socioeconomic strata.
But I had one exception in particular that now that you've asked the question, I never thought about it before.
That brings me to mind-- brings that particular instance to mind.

And maybe it has to be recorded because even though Bialystok was, in economic terms, a-- an outstanding example of self-governing and self-supporting community economically, there was plenty of poverty, obviously, even there and then, as it is in any community.
And this one person with whom I shared a bench in the school is-- is standing out as the antithesis of that-- of that milieu.
He was thin, drawn, and his right arm was paralyzed, but to be paralyzed from birth or paralyzed later, I don't know what.
It was fully grown but paralyzed.
He had to use his left hand to push the right hand to write.
So-- so this disabled, he was in terms of that in itself.
But apart from this physical disability, he was so poor.
And yet I made-- or maybe I made a unconscious effort of becoming a particular friend of his, so much so that I was the only friend whom he invited to his home.
He wouldn't have invited everybody else and for good reason.
I think Bachach was his name-- Bachach.
They lived in an attic.
The parents' bedroom was divided from the rest of the living space with a curtain.
There was no other beds in that room.
Where that-- he slept, I can't say.
Where they slept on some sort of a so-called mattress on the floor at nighttime, which was rolled out or not, I can't say.
The-- the-- the bits of-- the sticks of furniture was so-- so few that it was pitiful.
He was obviously a-- a boy who most probably didn't have sufficient [INAUDIBLE] to eat.
And yet he was a very, very brilliant, very capable.
And it was part of that same class situation.
And you just could not-- he was mindful of it.
He was reserved.

He was withdrawn, knowing not only his physical disability but also his-- his-- his status disability because most probably his father had only at best temporary work with only different that here when you don't have work, you are going on the dole.
There was no such thing as dole in pre-war Poland or, for that matter, not much else in-- in-- in Europe.
And if it was, it was-- it was pitiful.

So it just brings to mind the-- the-- the-- the difference of-- of this-- of the situation that-- that made our normal state of-- of affairs, which was nothing but at best adequate under situation of-- of-- of-- of house and-- and factory and everything woven together and so on, which was abnormal and so on.
But it looked like a palace by comparison when I looked at-- at what this young man was putting up with.
So that is one aspect of it, just to show the contrast.
The other-- the other thing was that as soon as I finished school-- and at school, I was enrolled.
I was first-- in the first year I arrived at school in Europe, 8, they immediately elected me as chairman of the class committee.
And by the year 10, I was the chairman of the Self Help Committee of the school, which was the only activity the school had on their all-school basis.
And they picked on me to be the chairman for whatever reasons.
And-- and I was there till the very-- the very end.
The-- but as soon as I left school, when I still was in Bialystok, I was immediately involved in community especially the Zionist movement-- not the youth movement but the adult movement.

So right into adult movement, enrolling myself in the politics of the-- in the-- in that [POLISH] of-- of Bialystok, so to speak, of the election campaigns for the-- for the [POLISH], which was a-- a-- a fight, a political fight against the [POLISH], against the religious, and against the-- and with all the youthful enthusiasm and-- and innocence that went with it, I used to involve myself with all of these sort of things.
So those were the friends.
I was always the youngest in any situation.
Never looked for friends of my own age.
I always looked for people older than I.
And I fitted in very, very comfortably.
I felt at home among older people.
The-- the more serious the situation was, the more at home I felt.
At school, they used to call me [POLISH], the walking seriousness.
[LAUGHS]
Only in terms of-- of general-- of general self-awareness, rather than-- than-- than an outgoing act.
When you used to go to the-- to the taxation of his-- which, of course, a government instrumentality, and-- and we were the taxpayers and the supplicants.
So we were at the wrong end of the stick in the situation.

But I can't say that I have personally-- excuse me-- encountered this sort of thing until, of course, until my student days in Warsaw because-- and that may be appropriate to mention at this stage because you are asking me explicitly about anti-Semitism.
Being a student at the university level, we were entitled to be-- to be serving in the officers corps-- the cadet corp, officers corp.
And we were duty-bound by law to-- to offer ourselves for selection.
And we presented ourselves.
And contrary to all normal procedure, when everybody was trying to get out of things in the army and so on, so forth, all the questions we answered was in the positive.
Are you well?
Yes.
Are you healthy?
Did you have any disabilities?
No.
I-- everything was answered positively because we knew beforehand that we, as Jews, we would never be accepted into the officers corps.
So we always got a number above the norm and sent home free without any commitment.
There were no-- there were no-- no Jewish students in-- in the late '30s included or accepted in the officers school in Poland because it was-- Hitler was well on the way.
And Poland used to take one leaf after another from Hitler's book by then.
Yes, sure, of course.
It is rather a-- a-- a-- a particular thing worth-- worth mentioning, worth describing because as I said in the beginning of the interview that it was a city of 100,000 people.

And it was economically self-- self-supportive in the sense that it had a core-- core industries that gave the-- the population a economic base and a tempo of life and a rhythm of life and a-- that-- that-- that was not available to many or most of-- of Jewish hamlets and townships.
We talked about the shtetl.
The shtetl is [INAUDIBLE] of-- of something otherworldly almost in Jewish-- Yiddish literature and so on.

But all that-- all that description, all that beauty that is attached with it-- and I made slight reference to it when I mentioned my mother's birthplace of [POLISH]-- which was-- that precisely because there was not much material basis, then-- then you had to fill the life with other venues.
You had to fill with other-- with other aims, with other ambitions.
And the man-- the leader-- the creator of the Habima in Israel, the-- the national theater, was born in Horodok.
He was a friend of my-- of my mother's because that was-- they-- they concentrated on things other-- otherworldly because the worldly offerings were limited or almost non-existent.

So to-- for-- to-- in that sense, the poverty, and I had an encounter with that poverty when I traveled to Poland on my father's behalf to collect-- to collect, if possible, non-- the non-- non-honored promissory notes that were coming back for good that people have bought from us and never had a chance of paying for.

And I had an encounter to see Jewish existence in those small townships and hamlets in the role, as it was, not as you read it in a book or as it being [INAUDIBLE] by-- by a writer who wants to put the-- the best-- the best face on it and so on.
And I've got pictures in my mind of Jewish shops-- little shops next to one another with the [HEBREW] people in their little beards and little Hasidic caps, standing there, pale faced, drawn.

The few pieces of merchandise that was on their-- on their shelves were put in such a way to cover the emptiness of the shelves so that there is some sort-- and those pieces-- a few pieces of rolls of material there did not belong to them.
It wasn't theirs.
Like the-- the ones that we gave them, they took on credit and never-- and never could pay for them.
Never could pay for them.
If-- if they managed to put one meal a day on the table, they were doing well.
So hopeless, some of the life of these people.

And that's why people emigrated in such numbers in the-- in the end of last century and the beginning of this century if there was opportunity to go anywhere because-- because the-- the-- the-- the economic base in most places was very, very unstable, if at all.
And you lived-- the-- the-- the-- the population was predominantly agrarian, illiterate, poor.
They couldn't afford if-- if a-- if a peasant bought himself a coat, it had to last him for a lifetime.
If he had a pair of shoes, he didn't put it on on Sunday to go to church from the village.
He used to carry it on his shoulders.
And he came to the church precinct.
And there, before he entered the church, he put on the shoes.
Never mind the socks.
He didn't have socks.
He had a bit of cloth to put around his-- his feet and then put on the shoes.
And the minute he came out of the church, he took off his shoes.
And there were parts of Poland where the peasants had to divide a match in two because he couldn't afford to waste the whole match in one go.
So, so poor were the population.
And what sort of a potential such poverty had for an economy of a country of 33 million people.
So in this respect, Bialystok was a very wealthy [INAUDIBLE] because although it served the local market, but it also traded with overseas.
It exported its early products as far away as the Far East, as far away as [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
When I, in my travels, I came across it.
So-- so we-- Bialystok used to import goods from the West as whole material.
And because we also did not use a fine wool as we know it in-- in Australia, a sheep's wool in the first place, a rolled wool for manufacture-- they used second-hand wool.
They used old cloth that they used the-- the old socks and old wooden cloth, they used to bring, sort, and then tear it to shreds again.
And out of these shreds they used to make new material.
And-- and-- and what we used to produce there was blankets, army-- army uniforms or-- or-- or-- or army coats, the heavy army coats, and other forms of-- of-- of material.
With time, of course, it has improved.
But even so, it had-- it had-- it had a full integrated industry.
It was a integrated.
There-- there were spinning mills.
There were dye houses.
There were-- there were looms and-- that-- that used to-- to-- to-- to do the actual.
And the people who ran the looms were many of them-- many of them were outdoor people.
They had the loom put away in one of the rooms and take out some work so that the big manufacturer didn't have to-- to-- to keep big factories.
Some-- some did.
But-- but there was a great deal of other work.
When there was work, there was work.
If not, you had to wait until the work came.
How they managed in meantime, God only knows.
So that was the nature of Bialystok.

And then, of course, being so-- so-- so self-assertive terms economic, they also-- and they had also their banks, their own co-operative banks to help the people in the trade to bridge both the shortage of cash because the entire economy was on promissory notes.
You used to run a business on promissory notes of 18 months.
In the early days of Israel, of Palestine, the situation was exactly the same.
And the people who came from Russia and from Poland and from Romania to Israel didn't know anything else.
There was no ready cash, so the entire economy was based on long-term promissory notes.

And the-- the banks that used to do it, who came into the existence, were the people who used to discount those long-term promissory notes to give the holders ready cash with which to pay wages and to-- and to make ends meet until such time that the cycle turned around in full.
And then the next-- the next product came along.
And the new promissory notes came into-- and then you start praying every day that-- that by the time that the-- the date is due that they will find the money there to pay for it.
So that was the-- and then, of course, there was a great, a very developed social and political activity.
Every aspect of-- of life.
And-- and it was all voluntary, paramedical and medical in the Jewish hospital and-- and-- and the different parties and different institutions and clubs and [INAUDIBLE] vote and schools and so on.
And it was all Jewish.
It was all Jewish.
And culturally?
And culturally-- culturally, it was a-- it was a-- a-- a Garden of Eden.
And that's why when I first arrived here, from the Jewish point of view, I arrived in a desert.

I arrived in a desert because one of the first questions that they asked me when I arrived in-- in this country, and I was at [POLISH] home, the first question that I asked them, how many Jewish day schools have you got here?
And the answer was none.
So within 48 hours of my arrival in Australia, I knew what I had to do.
Well, I think I have mentioned it in passing before.
We were aware.
We couldn't not be aware because first of all, there was a press.
And there was already a radio.
Radio was a, over there, a common-- a common means of communication.
And since Hitler came to power in 1933, the writing was on the wall.
Nobody, of course, anticipated to what degrees, and where that tragedy will end, and how it will end.

But-- but everybody knew what was happening because it's the first acts of Hitler's regime was to take the-- the-- the people who were non-Germans Jewish citizen living in Germany and send them across the border into Poland.
The first thing we knew is that we had to set up on the Polish-- inside the Polish border-- camps to hold all those people who were evicted from Germany, not matter how long they lived there.
And that was only the first-- the first act.
Then came the Kristallnacht, with-- with-- with the burning of the synagogues and so on.
And then came the-- the economic boycotts of the Jews of Germany.
And then came the throwing out of the people from their positions and so on.

It was an unfolding picture, even for those who did not read You didn't have to go and read Hitler's to know what he really intended to do because you could read a book and say that he has written it.
But it doesn't mean that he was going to do it.
But he was as good as his word.
The tragedy was that he was the worst at his word.
No.
Nothing.
Nothing, and it was not only that we as a family didn't [INAUDIBLE].
The-- the objective situation of the Jewish people in Europe-- and I'm not talking only of Bialystok or Poland.
The objective situation of the Jewish people of Europe was such that-- excuse me-- that realistically speaking, there was no plans to make.
Where could you go?
America, but that times, the United States had quotas for each country.
And the quotas were booked out years in advance.
So no country would-- would accept you.
No country would take you.
You know, the-- the-- the-- the-- the infamous conference in 1937 at the [INAUDIBLE] palace and so on where-- where people came.
And-- and even-- even Australia was present there.
That is a Colonel White made a statement that there was no room in Australia for-- for-- for Jewish refugees.
Nobody.
Nobody would let anybody in.
And if they would've let in 1,000 to 2,000 people, Poland alone had 3 million people.
There are 6 million people in Europe.
So it was-- and-- and-- and there is nothing.
And where could you go?
The-- you live from hand to mouth economically.
The-- it's not the case that you are-- you are transferring your-- your-- your-- your wealth or your bank account, and you shift another country by you.
The only-- the only possibility was Palestine in those days.
And that must be accentuated.
And maybe that is the answer, the most-- the clearest answer to your question.
According to the rules of the mandate-- of the British mandate on Palestine, if you could produce 1,000 pounds, you got considered a capitalist.
And then you were entitled to a capitalist certificate of entry into the country.
It was unlimited.
If you can produce 1,000 pounds-- 1,000 pounds was $5,000-- not only people in Bialystok, but take people in-- in Germany itself.
In Germany, under Hitler's rule, there were plenty of German people who could bring together 1,000 pounds and-- and get the certificate and go.
They didn't do it.
They didn't do it because nobody believed that it's going to happen.

They were-- they were sitting there like sitting ducks, even when at-- at-- even those who have left Poland to went to Lithuania to-- to escape the immediate war thought that by going away from Poland to Lithuania to Vilna, which was already then handed over by the Russians to as the capital of Lithuania, they thought, it's far enough to outlive the war years there.
So short-sighted and so naive were the people.
And they were people who were otherwise were business people and smart people and so on.
And they wouldn't budge.
They wouldn't even try and attempt to get the visa.
Didn't want to go way too far from home because they've left factories behind.
They've left real estate behind.
They've left things like that.
And they didn't budge.
And they-- and they perished.
Well, it was-- it was a matter of-- of just utterances of-- of-- of individual pessimism.
It-- it was only a matter how pessimistic one was.
I, for one, strangely enough, took very early a very pessimistic view.
And I remember-- and that maybe answers some of the very early questions that you have asked me about my relationship with my father.

After the Germans came in first time and left and handed over to the Russians, and the Russians-- and then-- and then there was a moment when everybody was buying everything because the money was losing [INAUDIBLE] value and so.
And I pleaded with my father and said, you lived through a war.
You were-- all right.
It was a different Germany.
But you were occupied by the Germans in the First World War.
It so happened that when I came back over the border, and I've heard that [POLISH] is still in town, and I went to plead with him to go, to have-- to have-- to have a pity on himself.
I persuaded.
I went together with Shmuel Ivry, who is-- who is a emeritus professor in Baltimore today.
He listened to us.
I made Shmuel Ivry come with me to plead with him.
The night after he left, the next night they came for him.
He escaped to Vilna.
We were in Vilna together and so on.
So-- and those are clever people.
He was a lawyer of-- outstanding lawyer.
Blinded.
Didn't want to move.
And I said to him, why?
He said, look, my dear fellow.
When the-- when the-- when the maid brings you a cup of coffee and biscuits in the morning in bed, you are not inclined to become a refugee.
That was the answer that I got.
And I was then 19 years old.
Thank you.
20 Years old.
